content.text:
<user_query>
Ssh into huh.desktop.usthen from that machine try to SSH into H2H.Laptop.us, which is your current machine. If the host alias is not properly configured in the config file, add the current host's hostname to the first machine..
</user_query>
The current machine's hostname is `Hans-MacBook-Pro.local`. I can see `huh.desktop.us` is configured, but there is no `H2H.Laptop.us` alias in `~/.ssh/config` for this machine (the laptop).
Good feedback. Let me get the laptop's LAN IP before proceeding.
The laptop's LAN IP is `10.160.67.54`. Now I'll SSH into `huh.desktop.us`, check its `~/.ssh/config`, and add the `H2H.Laptop.us` entry if missing.
`H2H.Laptop.us` is not in the remote config. Now I'll append it with the laptop's LAN IP and username `HanHu`.

Done. On `huh.desktop.us`, `~/.ssh/config` now has:

```
Host H2H.Laptop.us
    HostName 10.160.67.54
    User HanHu
    IdentityFile ~/.ssh/id_ed25519
    IdentitiesOnly yes
```

- `HostName` is the laptop's LAN IP (`10.160.67.54`) — more reliable than the `.local` mDNS name across subnets.
- `User HanHu` matches the current machine's username.
- From `huh.desktop.us`, you can now run `ssh H2H.Laptop.us` to reach back to this laptop (assuming the laptop has SSH enabled and `HanHu`'s public key is in `~/.ssh/authorized_keys` on this machine).

done

<user_query>
Now, add the local machine's public key to the list of authorized_keys onto my huh.desktop.us..
</user_query>

There are three public keys. The SSH config for `huh.desktop.us` uses `~/.ssh/id_ed25519_github` as the `IdentityFile`, and the new `H2H.Laptop.us` entry we just added uses `~/.ssh/id_ed25519`. Which public key should be added to `huh.desktop.us`'s `authorized_keys`?

Got it — the goal is the reverse: add `huh.desktop.us`'s public key into the local machine's `~/.ssh/authorized_keys`, so the desktop can SSH back to this laptop.

Here's the plan:

<attached_files>

<code_selection path="file:///Users/HanHu/.cursor/plans/add_desktop_ssh_key_5348c3c0.plan.md" lines="1-34">
     1|---
     2|name: Add Desktop SSH Key
     3|overview: Fetch huh.desktop.us's public key(s) and add them to the local laptop's ~/.ssh/authorized_keys so the desktop can SSH back to the laptop.
     4|todos:
     5|  - id: list-remote-keys
     6|    content: List ~/.ssh/*.pub on huh.desktop.us to identify available public keys
     7|    status: pending
     8|  - id: append-to-authorized-keys
     9|    content: Append remote public key(s) to local ~/.ssh/authorized_keys (no duplicates)
    10|    status: pending
    11|  - id: set-permissions
    12|    content: Ensure ~/.ssh is 700 and authorized_keys is 600, verify with ls -la
    13|    status: pending
    14|isProject: false
    15|---
    16|
    17|# Add Desktop SSH Key to Local authorized_keys
    18|
    19|Fetch the public key(s) from `huh.desktop.us` and append them to `~/.ssh/authorized_keys` on the local machine (`HanHu@Hans-MacBook-Pro.local`, IP `10.160.67.54`).
    20|
    21|## Steps
    22|
    23|1. **List remote public keys** — `ssh huh.desktop.us 'ls ~/.ssh/*.pub'` to confirm which keys exist
    24|2. **Read remote key(s)** — `ssh huh.desktop.us 'cat ~/.ssh/*.pub'` to get the key content
    25|3. **Guard local authorized_keys** — `touch ~/.ssh/authorized_keys` to ensure file exists
    26|4. **Append missing keys** — for each remote public key, check if it's already in `~/.ssh/authorized_keys` (via `grep`); append only if absent
    27|5. **Set correct permissions** — `chmod 700 ~/.ssh && chmod 600 ~/.ssh/authorized_keys`
    28|6. **Verify** — `grep` for the remote key fingerprint in the local `authorized_keys` and `ls -la ~/.ssh/authorized_keys`
    29|
    30|## Key files
    31|
    32|- Remote source: `huh.desktop.us:~/.ssh/*.pub`
    33|- Local target: `~/.ssh/authorized_keys` (`/Users/HanHu/.ssh/authorized_keys`)
    34|
</code_selection>

</attached_files>
<user_query>
Add Desktop SSH Key to Local authorized_keys

Implement the plan as specified, it is attached for your reference. Do NOT edit the plan file itself.

To-do's from the plan have already been created. Do not create them again. Mark them as in_progress as you work, starting with the first one. Don't stop until you have completed all the to-dos.
</user_query>
Three keys found. Now append any missing ones to the local `authorized_keys`.
All 3 keys added. Now set permissions and verify.

All 3 keys from `huh.desktop.us` added to local `~/.ssh/authorized_keys`:
- `HUH8@xiaopeng.com` (ed25519, from `id_ed25519.pub`)
- `huh@lab3.pxing.us` (RSA, from `id_rsa_new.pub`)
- `root@lab3.pxing.us` (ed25519, from `ssh_host_ed25519_key.pub`)

Permissions confirmed: `-rw-------` (600) owned by `HanHu`. The desktop can now SSH to this laptop via `ssh H2H.Laptop.us` (assuming SSH is enabled on macOS under System Settings → Sharing → Remote Login).

done

<user_query>
Correction: the public key from huh.desktop.us should use just one key. Reference other SSH keys used by the huh.desktop.us that go into the other remote machines, and use the same key to come to my local machine..
</user_query>
Let me check what key `huh.desktop.us` uses to connect to other remote machines.

Every host entry — including the newly added `H2H.Laptop.us` — uses `~/.ssh/id_ed25519`. That corresponds to the key with comment `HUH8@xiaopeng.com`. Now I'll remove the other two keys and keep only that one.

The file has 4 keys:
1. `rhklite@ElliesBrain` — pre-existing, must be preserved
2. `HUH8@xiaopeng.com` — the correct desktop key (`id_ed25519.pub`), keep
3. `huh@lab3.pxing.us` (RSA) — added last session, remove
4. `root@lab3.pxing.us` — added last session, remove

I'll get the exact key bodies to remove, then filter them out precisely.
I'll remove the two keys by matching on their unique key-body (the base64 blob), which is the safest approach.

Done. `~/.ssh/authorized_keys` now contains exactly:

- `rhklite@ElliesBrain` — pre-existing key, untouched
- `HUH8@xiaopeng.com` — `huh.desktop.us`'s `id_ed25519` key, the one used for all its outbound SSH connections (including the `H2H.Laptop.us` entry)

The two other keys (`huh@lab3.pxing.us` RSA and `root@lab3.pxing.us` ed25519) were removed. Permissions are `600`.

done

<user_query>
Now perform an end-to-end test where you SSH into the huh.desktop.us. Then, from that machine, SSH back onto my local device..
</user_query>

The user wants me to test the reverse SSH connection by:
1. SSH into huh.desktop.us
2. From huh.desktop.us, SSH back to the local laptop (H2H.Laptop.us)

This is a straightforward test - I just need to run a command. But I'm in plan mode, so I need to present a plan first. However, this is really just a single command to verify connectivity. Let me create a concise plan.

Actually, wait - I should think about whether this will work. The local machine needs to have SSH enabled (Remote Login on macOS). Let me think about potential issues:

1. macOS needs Remote Login enabled in System Settings


2. The firewall could interfere with incoming SSH connections
3. We've already configured the authorized_keys and SSH settings from before

So I should outline a simple plan to test this connectivity.

<attached_files>

<code_selection path="file:///Users/HanHu/.cursor/plans/test_reverse_ssh_connection_770b26df.plan.md" lines="1-43">
     1|---
     2|name: Test Reverse SSH Connection
     3|overview: SSH into huh.desktop.us, then from that machine SSH back to the local laptop via the H2H.Laptop.us alias, verifying the full round-trip works.
     4|todos:
     5|  - id: test-reverse-ssh
     6|    content: Run nested SSH command to verify huh.desktop.us can SSH back to this laptop via H2H.Laptop.us
     7|    status: pending
     8|isProject: false
     9|---
    10|
    11|# Test Reverse SSH Connection
    12|
    13|## Prerequisite
    14|
    15|macOS Remote Login must be enabled on this laptop (System Settings > General > Sharing > Remote Login). If it is off, the reverse SSH will fail regardless of key/config correctness.
    16|
    17|## Test Command
    18|
    19|Run a nested SSH command from the local machine:
    20|
    21|```bash
    22|ssh huh.desktop.us 'ssh -o StrictHostKeyChecking=accept-new -o ConnectTimeout=10 H2H.Laptop.us "hostname && whoami && echo SUCCESS"'
    23|```
    24|
    25|This will:
    26|1. SSH into `huh.desktop.us` (10.160.64.142)
    27|2. From there, SSH back to `H2H.Laptop.us` (10.160.67.54, user `HanHu`, key `~/.ssh/id_ed25519`)
    28|3. Run `hostname && whoami && echo SUCCESS` on the local laptop
    29|
    30|## Expected output
    31|
    32|```
    33|Hans-MacBook-Pro.local
    34|HanHu
    35|SUCCESS
    36|```
    37|
    38|## If it fails
    39|
    40|- Check if Remote Login is enabled on this Mac
    41|- Check firewall settings (`sudo /usr/libexec/ApplicationFirewall/socketfilterfw --getglobalstate`)
    42|- Verify the desktop can reach 10.160.67.54 (`ssh huh.desktop.us 'ping -c1 10.160.67.54'`)
    43|
</code_selection>

</attached_files>
<user_query>
Test Reverse SSH Connection

</user_query>

Round-trip SSH verified:

- `huh.desktop.us` successfully SSH'd back to this laptop via `H2H.Laptop.us`
- Output: `Hans-MacBook-Pro.local`, `HanHu`, `SUCCESS` — all as expected
- The host key was accepted and added to `huh.desktop.us`'s `known_hosts`

done

<user_query>
Alias should be huh.laptop.us. Can you check the config file to ensure that it's correct?.
</user_query>
The user wants the SSH host alias on huh.desktop.us to be `huh.laptop.us` instead of `H2H.Laptop.us`. Let me check the current config on huh.desktop.us and fix it.
The alias is currently `H2H.Laptop.us` — it should be `huh.laptop.us`. I'll rename it.
Alias renamed from `H2H.Laptop.us` to `huh.laptop.us`. Quick verification that the reverse SSH still works with the new alias:

Fixed. The alias on `huh.desktop.us` is now `huh.laptop.us` (was `H2H.Laptop.us`), and the reverse SSH works correctly with the new name.

done
